docs.title: MC-45 SPOT DEPOSITOR
docs.text: Version 1.0 / Model MC-45 LH and RH / Date: June 2026 / Serial Number: [_______]

docs.title: 1.1 Emergency Stop Devices
docs.text:
Three emergency stop devices are installed on the Depositor: one on the OI enclosure, one on the ELECTRICAL ENCLOSURE, and one on the FEED-Z CONVEYOR (option). When pressed, the device removes all motion from the Depositor. To reset, pull the actuator to release.

Figure 1.1a Emergency Stop Locations

Figure 1.1b Emergency Stop Locations

For the status of all emergency stop devices and guard switches, navigate to the Safety PLC screen on the OI. See Section 12: OI Reference.

docs.title: 1.2 Guard Switches
docs.text:
Seven safety guard switches are monitored by the safety PLC. The Depositor will not run if any guard is open or removed.

Switch Location ENTRY Entry guard MIDDLE Middle guard EXIT Exit guard PORTION FRONT Portion belt assembly PORTION BACK Portion belt assembly SPINNER Spinner area HOPPER Hopper area

Figure 1.2a Guard Switch Locations

Figure 1.2b Guard Switch Locations

docs.title: 1.3 Safe State Definition
docs.text:
Pressing an emergency stop device or opening any guard switch places the Depositor in a safe state. The following occur automatically:

  • All motion commands are removed. Motors coast to stop.
  • The RETRACTABLE CONVEYOR servo drive is disabled.
  • Pneumatic air supply to the BUCKET cylinder is removed.

The MACHINE ENABLE circuit must be reset before operation can resume. See Section 3: Startup.

Note

Emergency stop devices are for emergency conditions only. For normal shutdown, use the STOP control on the HOME screen.

docs.title: 2.1 Component Identification
docs.text:
The diagrams below identify all major components.

Figure 2.1a Primary components, callouts [1]–[11] # Component 1 PRODUCT CONVEYOR 2 PRODUCT CONVEYOR ENCODER 3 PORTION CONVEYOR 4 SPINNER MOTOR 5 RAKE MOTOR 6 BUCKET 7 CARPET 8 PORTION CONVEYOR MOTOR 9 TARGET SENSOR 10 EXIT GUARD 11 OPERATOR INTERFACE

Figure 2.1b Primary components, callouts [12]–[23] # Component 12 MIDDLE GUARD 13 ENTRY GUARD 14 PORTION GUARD (FRONT) 15 PORTION GUARD (BACK) 16 AIR SUPPLY 17 HOPPER 18 CONTROL ENCLOSURE 19 FEED-Z CONTROL ENCLOSURE (Option) 20 FEED-Z GUARD (Option) 21 FEED-Z CONVEYOR MOTOR (Option) 22 RAKE GUARD 23 RAKE HEIGHT INDICATOR

Note

Component descriptions are in Appendix B: Glossary. Control panel layout is in Section 2.2: Control Panel Layout.

docs.title: 2.2 Control Panel Layout
docs.text: Figure 2.2  Control Enclosure Layout # Component 1 ELECTRICAL DISCONNECT 2 CIRCUIT BREAKERS 3 VARIABLE FREQUENCY DRIVES (VFD) 4 SERVO DRIVES 5 SAFETY CONTACTORS AND SAFETY PLC 6 ANALOG SIGNAL CONDITIONER 7 POWER SUPPLY 8 NETWORK SWITCHES 9 WIRE TERMINALS 10 PLC AND I/O 11 TRANSFORMER

docs.title: 3.1 Startup Sequence
docs.text:
Figure 3.1 Startup sequence flowchart

  1. Confirm the Depositor is fully reassembled. Belts, guards, and scrapers are all in place.

  2. Verify the compressed air supply is ON.

  3. Turn the ELECTRICAL DISCONNECT to the ON position. Wait for the PLC and OI to finish initializing. The HOME screen appears when ready. If active alarms are present, the OI displays the ALARMS screen first. Navigate to the ALARMS screen, correct all conditions, then press FAULT RESET once to clear all resettable faults.

    Note

    If the MACHINE ENABLE indicator flashes: pull all E-STOP actuators to release, close all guards, then press MACHINE ENABLE again. If it continues to flash, navigate to MANUAL → SAFETY PLC to identify which device remains open. Faults that do not clear after pressing FAULT RESET require device-specific corrective action. Navigate to the ALARMS screen and refer to Section 13: Troubleshooting for guidance.

  4. Press the MACHINE ENABLE PUSH BUTTON. Verify the MACHINE ENABLE indicator is illuminated solid.

  5. When all criteria are satisfied, proceed to Section 4: Operating Modes.

docs.title: 4  Operating Modes
docs.text: Sections 4.3 through 4.6 describe the four operator-selected modes. PRIME and CONSERVE are automatic states. The Depositor enters PRIME and CONSERVE modes based on operating conditions.

docs.title: 4.1 Depositor Control: LOCAL and REMOTE
docs.text:
Figure 4.1 LOCAL and REMOTE control flowchart
  1. LOCAL control: Start and stop the Depositor from the HOME screen using PRESS TO START and STOP. The Depositor runs independently of any upstream or downstream equipment.
  2. REMOTE control: An external line signal starts and stops the Depositor. A dry-contact output (Upstream Interlock) sends the Depositor's run status to upstream equipment.

docs.title: 4.2 Mode Selection
docs.text:
Figure 4.2 Operating mode selection flowchart
  1. Run Production. Select RUN mode for normal topping application. The Depositor verifies priming criteria before starting. If not met, the OI prompts the operator to complete PRIME mode first.
  2. Empty Depositor. Select EMPTY mode to clear topping from the Depositor.
  3. Bypass Depositor (Pass-Thru). Select BYPASS mode to run the PRODUCT CONVEYOR without applying topping.

docs.title: 4.3 PRIME / RUN Mode
docs.text: Figure 4.3  PRIME and RUN mode flowchart

docs.title: PRIME Mode
docs.text:
1. Select PRIME mode. Press and hold PRESS TO START for three seconds.
  2. The RAKE moves to the recipe position. Allow travel to complete before loading topping.
  3. Load topping manually at the HOPPER.
  4. Priming continues until PORTION CONVEYOR weight exceeds 75% of TARGET LEVEL for 10 continuous seconds.
  5. When complete, the Depositor automatically transitions to RUN mode.

Note

PRIME mode is available only in LOCAL control. Selecting RUN mode before priming is complete prompts the OI to require PRIME mode to finish first.

docs.title: RUN Mode
docs.text:
When priming is complete, the Depositor transitions to RUN mode automatically. The PRODUCT CONVEYOR runs continuously. The RAKE, SPINNER, PORTION CONVEYOR, and FEED-Z CONVEYOR start when a target is detected.

The STATUS BANNER displays MACHINE RUNNING.

docs.title: 4.4 EMPTY Mode
docs.text:
Figure 4.4 EMPTY mode flowchart
  1. Select EMPTY mode. Press and hold PRESS TO START for three seconds. All conveyor drives run to clear topping from the system. Watch the HOPPER and RAKE area. When topping no longer recirculates and the belt surfaces are clear, press STOP.

Note

EMPTY mode does not stop automatically. Press STOP when emptying is complete. EMPTY mode is available in LOCAL control only.

docs.title: 4.5 BYPASS Mode
docs.text:
Figure 4.5 BYPASS mode flowchart
  1. Select BYPASS mode. Start the Depositor. The PRODUCT CONVEYOR operates without topping application.

Note

When equipped with a safety PLC, BYPASS mode allows guards to remain open. Contact Grote Service before running BYPASS with a guard open if the installed safety circuit type is not known.

docs.title: 4.6 CONSERVE Mode
docs.text:
Figure 4.6 CONSERVE mode flowchart
  1. During RUN mode, the Depositor monitors the TARGET SENSOR.
  2. If no target is detected, the CONSERVE timer starts. A target arriving before the timer expires resets the timer and operation continues normally.
  3. If the timer expires, the Depositor enters CONSERVE mode.
  4. All operations except the PRODUCT CONVEYOR pause: the RAKE, SPINNER, PORTION CONVEYOR, and FEED-Z CONVEYOR stop.
  5. The STATUS BANNER displays MACHINE RUNNING CONSERVE MODE ACTIVE.
  6. When a target is detected, CONSERVE mode clears automatically and all stopped motors restart.

Note

Set the CONSERVE timer at MACHINE OPTIONS → CONSERVE MODE TIME [ms], in milliseconds. The value should be based on travel time from the TARGET SENSOR to the drop point at the expected line speed.

docs.title: 5.1 Recipe Screen: Field Reference
docs.text:
Figure 5.1 RECIPE screen

All production parameters are stored in the active recipe. Editable fields have a yellow background with green text. See Section 12: OI Reference for screen layout and Appendix A: Recipe Starting Points for starting values.

Field Range Description RAKE SPEED 10–100 % RAKE motor speed. SPINNER SPEED 10–100 % SPINNER motor speed. PRODUCT CONV 0–60 FPM PRODUCT CONVEYOR speed. LINE RATE 0–48 PPM Target throughput in portions per minute. Used by the PLC to calculate PORTION CONVEYOR move speed. FEED-Z CONV 10–30 FPM FEED-Z CONVEYOR speed. (Option) FEED CONV 0–100 FPM FEED CONVEYOR speed. (Option) HOPPER LOW WEIGHT 0–40 lb PORTION CONVEYOR load cell lower threshold. FEED-Z CONVEYOR starts when weight falls below this value. HOPPER HIGH WEIGHT 0–40 lb PORTION CONVEYOR load cell upper threshold. FEED-Z CONVEYOR stops when weight exceeds this value. BUCKET OPEN TIME 0–1000 ms Time the BUCKET remains open on each deposit cycle. BUCKET FALL TIME 0–1000 ms Time allowed for topping to fall from the BUCKET to the target before the CARPET retracts. MASK OFFSET 0–14 in CARPET retracted position offset from home. Sets the starting position before each extend move. STROKE LENGTH 0–16 in CARPET extend distance from the retracted position. TARGET DELAY 0–3000 ms Time between TARGET SENSOR detection and CARPET trigger. EXTEND RATIO 0–2.0 Ratio of CARPET extend speed to retract speed. PORTION CONV MOVE LENGTH in Distance the PORTION CONVEYOR advances per machine cycle. PORTION CONV TIME 0–10000 ms Time window available for the PORTION CONVEYOR to complete its move. SKIP MODE ON / OFF When active, the Depositor deposits on every other target. See Section 5.3.

docs.title: 5.2 Loading and Saving Recipes
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the RECIPE MANAGEMENT screen on the OI.
  2. To load: enter the recipe number in RECIPE #. Press and hold LOAD RECIPE for three seconds.
  3. To create a new recipe: enter a recipe number and name. Enter all parameters. Press and hold SAVE RECIPE for three seconds.

Note

The Depositor stores up to 128 recipes. Saving to an existing recipe number overwrites it without confirmation. Verify the recipe number before saving.

docs.title: 5.3 Skip Mode
docs.text:
Skip Mode deposits topping on every other target. When active, the Depositor alternates between depositing and skipping each successive target.

To enable Skip Mode, set the SKIP MODE field to ON in the active recipe and save. The SKIP MODE indicator on the OI illuminates when active.

If the skip phase is out of sync with the line — depositing on targets that should be skipped — press SYNC on the OI. Each press shifts the skip phase by one target.

docs.title: 5.4 Rake Height Setup
docs.text:
RAKE HEIGHT is set manually using the hand-crank adjustment on the machine frame. The RAKE HEIGHT INDICATOR shows the current setting as a relative position number. This number has no engineering unit. It is a reference only.

Start from the baseline value in Appendix A: Recipe Starting Points for the topping type. Adjust in small increments. Allow approximately 100 targets to pass through before evaluating the bed after each adjustment.

docs.title: Topping Compaction Assessment
docs.text:
Before adjusting RAKE HEIGHT, assess the topping's compaction behavior:

  1. Collect a representative sample sufficient to fill one hand.
  2. Apply light pressure and open your hand. Poke the sample. If it separates freely, the topping is loose.
  3. Squeeze moderately and open your hand. Poke the sample. Does it fall apart or hold its shape?
  4. Squeeze firmly and open your hand. Try to break the sample apart. If shreds pull apart cleanly, compaction is not at the damage threshold. If the sample holds together and will not separate without tearing, that is the damage threshold. The bed must stay below this pressure level.
  5. Adjust RAKE HEIGHT in small increments. Allow approximately 100 targets to pass before evaluating.
  6. When the bed is uniform with no voids, save the recipe.

Compaction Assessment

High compaction (topping holds shape under light or moderate pressure): indicates high moisture or stickiness. Raise RAKE HEIGHT first to reduce tine engagement depth. A shallower tine bite reduces compressive force on the pile. Reduce HOPPER HIGH WEIGHT only if raising RAKE HEIGHT alone does not resolve compaction.

Low compaction (topping crumbles freely under firm pressure): start from the Appendix A baseline values. If the bed has voids, raise RAKE HEIGHT in small increments.

Both over-fill and under-fill produce the same symptom: voids and uneven coverage. If raising RAKE HEIGHT does not resolve voids, evaluate whether HOPPER HIGH WEIGHT is too high or too low before making further height adjustments.

Typical HOPPER HIGH WEIGHT ranges:

  • Shredded cheese (standard): 15–20 lb.
  • IQF / frozen product: 12–18 lb.
  • Mixed or vegetable toppings with varied piece sizes: similar to cheese range, with RAKE SPEED reduced.

Long or sticky shreds tend to bridge across the RAKE tines. The tines are staggered in two offset rows. A long shred can span the gap between tines and drag laterally as the RAKE turns. That dragging pulls material away and leaves a void in the bed. Raise RAKE HEIGHT to reduce tine engagement depth so shreds release rather than drag. Do not raise HOPPER HIGH WEIGHT to compensate: a heavier pile increases compaction pressure, which worsens the problem on sticky material. If voids persist after raising RAKE HEIGHT, reduce RAKE SPEED. RAKE SPEED should not exceed 60%.

Fine or short shreds behave more freely. The pile can be lighter, and RAKE HEIGHT can run lower.

Note

Repeat the assessment when the topping lot changes, after extended idle periods, or when ambient conditions differ significantly from normal.

docs.title: 6.1 Deposit Cycle Overview
docs.text:
The MC-45 deposits topping in a repeating cycle triggered by each target detected by the TARGET SENSOR. The cycle runs as follows:

  1. The PRODUCT CONVEYOR runs continuously. The TARGET SENSOR detects a target on the PRODUCT CONVEYOR.
  2. The TARGET DELAY elapses. The CARPET trigger fires.
  3. The CARPET extends from the MASK OFFSET position. It travels in sync with the target at a speed proportional to the PRODUCT CONVEYOR speed, set by the EXTEND RATIO recipe parameter. As the CARPET extends over the target, it folds mechanically to conform to the target surface, positioning the deposit zone over the border area.
  4. The CARPET retracts to the MASK OFFSET position.
  5. The PORTION CONVEYOR advances the length set by PORTION CONV MOVE LENGTH, loading the next portion of topping toward the BUCKET.
  6. The BUCKET FALL TIME elapses, allowing topping to fall from the PORTION CONVEYOR and reach the CARPET surface before the BUCKET opens.
  7. The BUCKET opens for the duration of BUCKET OPEN TIME. Topping falls through the MASK onto the CARPET. The SPINNER runs continuously and spreads the topping across the CARPET as it lands.
  8. The BUCKET closes. The CARPET is now loaded and ready for the next target.
  9. The cycle repeats on the next TARGET SENSOR detection.

In SKIP MODE, the CARPET trigger, BUCKET, and PORTION CONVEYOR all skip every other target. On a skipped target, the CARPET stays at the MASK OFFSET position, the BUCKET stays closed, and the PORTION CONVEYOR does not advance.

If the skip phase drifts out of alignment with the line — for example, when an operator removes a target to check deposit quality — press SYNC on the OI. Each press shifts the skip phase by one target, realigning the deposit pattern with the correct targets.

Figure 6.1 Deposit cycle sequence diagram Figure 6.1 Deposit cycle sequence diagram

docs.title: 6.2 CARPET Motion
docs.text:
The CARPET is a retractable belt that positions the target under the deposit zone. It does not return to the home position between cycles. Between cycles it holds at the MASK OFFSET position, which is an offset from home set in the active recipe.

MASK OFFSET sets where the CARPET starts its extend move, effectively aligning the deposit zone with the border of the target.

STROKE LENGTH controls the width of the deposit zone.

EXTEND RATIO controls how fast the CARPET travels relative to the PRODUCT CONVEYOR during the extend move.

The MASK is mounted between the BUCKET and the CARPET surface. It shapes the topping pattern — round masks produce round deposits, square cells produce square deposits — so the topping lands only within the border area defined by MASK OFFSET and STROKE LENGTH.

Figure 6.2 CARPET motion diagram showing home, offset, and extended positions Figure 6.2 CARPET motion diagram — home, offset, and extended positions

docs.title: 6.3 Target Tracking
docs.text: The Depositor tracks each target from the moment the TARGET SENSOR detects it through the deposit cycle. Three tracking modes are available, selected from the service screen on the OI.

docs.title: Time-Based Tracking
docs.text:
The PLC uses a five-timer memory chain to track the target's position after detection. Each timer represents a fixed time segment of the target's travel from the TARGET SENSOR to the CARPET trigger point. The delay between detection and trigger is set by TARGET DELAY in the active recipe.

Operators can fine-tune the trigger point from the HOME screen using the FINE and COARSE adjustment buttons, which update TARGET DELAY in the active recipe directly.

This mode requires no encoder. It is the standard production mode for most installations.

docs.title: Encoder-Based Tracking
docs.text:
When a PRODUCT CONVEYOR encoder is installed, the PLC tracks target position by distance rather than time. The encoder on the PRODUCT CONVEYOR shaft fires the CARPET trigger when the target has traveled the distance set by TARGET DELAY.

This mode compensates for line speed variations automatically. It is the preferred mode when conveyor speed changes frequently during production.

docs.title: Test Mode
docs.text:
Test Mode fires the CARPET trigger on a repeating timer interval set by TARGET MODE PRESET on the service screen. No TARGET SENSOR input is required. The CARPET cycles continuously at the set interval regardless of whether targets are present on the PRODUCT CONVEYOR.

Test Mode is used during commissioning and mechanical setup to verify CARPET motion and deposit placement without running product.

Figure 6.3 Target tracking mode selection diagram Figure 6.3 Target tracking mode selection diagram

docs.title: 6.4 HOPPER Fill Control
docs.text:
The RAKE maintains an even topping bed on the PORTION CONVEYOR. The RAKE runs continuously at the speed set by RAKE SPEED in the active recipe, leveling the bed to a consistent depth. As the BUCKET removes topping each cycle, the bed level drops. The HOPPER fill system replenishes topping automatically.

Two load cells monitor topping weight: one on the PORTION CONVEYOR (HOPPER) and one on the FEED CONVEYOR (when equipped). The HOPPER LOW WEIGHT and HOPPER HIGH WEIGHT recipe parameters set the start and stop thresholds for the FEED-Z CONVEYOR.

When HOPPER weight drops below HOPPER LOW WEIGHT, the FEED-Z CONVEYOR starts and delivers topping to the HOPPER area. When HOPPER weight reaches HOPPER HIGH WEIGHT, the FEED-Z CONVEYOR stops.

Figure 6.4 HOPPER fill control diagram showing load cell thresholds and FEED-Z CONVEYOR direction Figure 6.4 HOPPER fill control diagram

docs.title: 6.5 FEED-Z CONVEYOR — Two-Machine Configuration (Option)
docs.text:
When two MC-45 Depositors share one FEED-Z CONVEYOR, the conveyor is bidirectional. Running forward feeds MC45 Machine 1. Running in reverse feeds MC45 Machine 2.

Machine 1 takes priority. When both machines demand topping simultaneously, the FEED-Z CONVEYOR runs forward to serve Machine 1 first. Machine 2 waits until Machine 1 is satisfied or until the Machine 2 timeout expires. The timeout value is set from the OI.

The FEED CONVEYOR has its own load cell. The FEED CONVEYOR weight thresholds in the active recipe control FEED-Z CONVEYOR start and stop independently of the HOPPER load cell.

docs.title: 6.6 Supply and Demand Balance
docs.text:
The FEED-Z CONVEYOR cycles on and off to maintain topping weight between HOPPER LOW WEIGHT and HOPPER HIGH WEIGHT. The balance between topping consumption and replenishment rate determines how frequently the FEED-Z CONVEYOR runs and how stable the HOPPER weight stays during production.

Use the controls below to explore the relationship between line demand, HOPPER thresholds, and FEED-Z CONVEYOR cycle behavior. The FEED-Z BELT LEVEL slider represents the observed topping depth on the incline belt. Keeping the belt well loaded produces consistent flight fill and stable supply.

HOPPER Fill: Supply and Demand Balance Adjust line parameters and HOPPER thresholds to see FEED-Z CONVEYOR cycle behavior. Line Demand Machines 1 ◀ ▶ Targets / min (per machine) 22 ◀ ▶ Topping / target (oz) 8.0 ◀ ▶ Skip Mode OFF OFF HOPPER Weight Thresholds HIGH WEIGHT (lb) 20 ◀ ▶ LOW WEIGHT (lb) 10 ◀ ▶ FEED-Z Supply Belt speed (FPM) 19.4 ◀ ▶ Max lb / flight 1.25 ◀ ▶ 0.5 lb = light / fine  |  2.0 lb = dense product FEED-Z BELT LEVEL 6420 ▼ 4.0 in ▲ Flight load HOPPER Weight — Machine 1 0 lb 15.0 lb 40 lb ▮ HIGH ▮ LOW Satisfied HOPPER Weight — Machine 2 (Option) 0 lb 15.0 lb 40 lb ▮ HIGH ▮ LOW Satisfied Demand Supply Balance Demand Supply
